intent: enable secret scanning + push protection across every private GitHub repo I own in one shot instead of clicking through each repo's settings
finding: In the sandboxed-research-agent session the user twice asked to bulk-enable Secret Scanning and Push Protection on every private repo ('I want to set on all my private github repos: Enable secret scanning... Enable push protection... can you do this through gh script?'). They explicitly scoped the ask to automation ('can you do this through gh script?'). The recurrence surface is clear: every time the user spins up a new private repo, the same two toggles need to be flipped, and every time they re-evaluate their security posture they want the sweep re-run. A single durable artifact — a `gh api`/`gh repo edit` script or skill that enumerates private repos, enforces the two toggles, and reports drift — would replace both the one-off sweep and the per-new-repo manual step. Three-question test passes: outcome (all private repos have scanning+push protection), recurrence (per-new-repo and as a periodic audit), single artifact (gh script + cron / skill).
existing_mitigation: None found. No skill, script, or CLAUDE.md note wires `gh` to bulk-apply security settings. The user remarked public repos already get push protection by default — that only underscores that the private-repo gap is the automation target.

intent: auto-tour an Android app by driving it through every screen and capturing screenshots into a single reviewable artifact
finding: In two separate intender-app sessions the user opened with the same shape: 'I want a tour: show me images from every screen in the app so I can see what it looks like' and later 'Now I want those pictures. Please do a tour.' Both runs needed Claude to rediscover how to drive the emulator (install APK, skip onboarding, tap through gates with hard-coded screen coordinates, uiautomator dump, screenshot, reassemble into a per-screen set). The second session also hit a reproducible bug where shell stdout was being prepended to the PNG binary. The outcome — 'here's what every screen currently looks like' — is what the user wants on demand, and it will recur on every UI change, every new app, and every review. A single `android-tour` skill (or reused MCP + small orchestrator) that takes an app id / route list and returns a tagged gallery would replace the re-derivation each time. This is the generalisation of OBS-2026-04-18-001 (end-to-end testing on docker-android) but scoped narrower: the 'screenshot every screen for review' use case.
existing_mitigation: An Android MCP and docker-android setup exist and were used in both sessions; the orchestration 'drive app through every screen + collect screenshots cleanly' is not wrapped as a skill or script.

intent: stand up a locked-down web-research subagent (MCPs, domain allowlist, untrusted-output wrapping, git-auth plumbing) in a new environment without hand-assembling every piece
finding: Across two long sessions the user built and rebuilt the same stack: a sandboxed agent that does web research via exa/tavli MCPs, writes reports to a known path, and is the only component with network access. They walked Claude through domain allowlists in init-firewall.sh, untrusted-output wrapping of reports, keyring-based secret storage, pre-spin to kill startup overhead, fast/normal/deep tiers, and — in the adjacent session — short-lived GitHub App tokens so the sandboxed agent can push. The user's own words: 'I want this setup to live in its own repo... based on trailofbits devcontainer' and 'I want you to do everything that you can do and only ask me to do things that you cannot do.' Each new machine or fresh clone will need the same setup dance. A bootstrap script + spec that reads a config (which MCPs, which domains, which repo surfaces) and provisions the devcontainer, firewall rules, keyring hooks, and token rotation in one pass would convert a multi-hour manual assembly into a single `init` invocation.
existing_mitigation: `locked-down-headless-agent` skill exists and was updated with related learnings in earlier sessions; it does not yet bootstrap the firewall + keyring + MCP + token-rotation chain end-to-end from a config.

intent: split a big uncommitted change into logical-unit commits (spec / infra / app) before pushing
finding: The user said 'push the current, split it up into commits, one for any spec changes, one for the setup and MCP instructions, and one for the app itself.' This is a specific, named pattern — not a vague ask. It mirrors prior observations about batching large uncommitted piles into logical commits, but here the user gave the grouping scheme explicitly: spec / infra-and-tooling / app code. A `split-commits` skill that takes a grouping spec (by path glob, by file-type heuristic, or by diff-section labels) and proposes staged commits would replace the hand-sorting each time. One strong instance in this window plus adjacency to the earlier dotfiles observations — logging as a low-severity building block per categories.md guidance to err broad within the zone.
existing_mitigation: None found. `dotfiles/sync-agent.sh` handles a related dotfiles-specific case; no general commit-splitter. Existing `caveman-commit` skill generates commit messages but does not split a diff into logical groups.
